Councilor Miller. Thank you, Mayor. I do have a couple of additional items I'd like to add under my report. And for that, I would like to alert people in our community that the RM of St. Clements has a subdivision that's coming to their council. I believe on the 21st of January and it is specifically to do with what's called Cooke's Creek subdivision. Cooke's Creek estate subdivision, it's for 150 lots, just north of Highway 44. And while this may not directly affect us, of course, at this council, I have had some inquiries of people who live on Cook's Creek Road as well as in my neighborhood. So to get that information for those people who are interested, they should go directly to the St. Clements, RM or the RM of St. Clements website. And I believe there's a petition that's being circulated. I'm not saying to be in favor or against it, but all the information for that application is on the RM of St. Clements website. but because it's a very significant new development, I mean, 150 lots in a kind of rural agriculture area is kind of unique. I also wanted to note that they headingly put in a new rec center and their new rec center is really fantastic. It's got a gymnasium, a weight room. I think some of us toured that when we were at the arm meeting, but the cost to that rec center was $6 million. And I think very efficiently and very much responded to the community needs. And I think it's important for us and for our social or our sports director, so to speak, to know about this because I'm sure he does. But if they can do a rec center for $6 million, that's really accommodative to the people, you should also reflect on what we're doing too. I also, a couple more points quick. The European Union has now implemented what's called a PFAS water drinking water requirement. So in their regulation in the European Union, Union, all drinking water that's provided to citizens has to be tested for PFAS chemicals. And I say this because I'm a strong advocate internationally and I know North America doesn't take this very seriously, but the European Union does. So all the countries who are members of the European Union, which is I'm sure you can well imagine who they are, have this new requirement. And I'm hopeful that Canada, the United States and other countries take on this because PFAS are carcinogens, they're cancer causing, and they're prevalent in our drinking water. And so we ought to be taking serious notice of this. The, actually the next item I want to talk about is the arm of McDonald's has opted out of the WMR or the Metropolitan region. We had an opportunity, Councillor Cajinsky and I and Councillor Fuel to talk to representatives, our councillors from the RM of McDonald and they say it was the right thing for them to do to save taxpayers' repairs money. And they didn't want to be bound by the requirements of that organization that they would have to commit to whatever certain amount of years. As you know, it costs us 16 to $17,000 a year and we haven't had to face the reality of that yet, but we will probably in the months to come. So we'll have to make a decision on that, what's best for our residents, but we should also be engaging with them to provide them inside of what is being planned with that and how we can utilize them going forward. And I also wanted to mention the city want to pay as well as the arm of Reynolds have already posted information about the 2026 election. And so I wanted to ask our CAO what's the anticipated timelines for us if RM is already doing that some of our neighbors. We as you know have an election here in October of this year, what is the framework for this for our RM? Well, I would say by March we'll have our website updated with everything for 2020.

A model built on centralized authority, minimal public input, and unelected structures making decisions above local governments. on December 16th, 2025, the Romanist's Palli of McDonald exercised that authority by council resolution and formally withdrew from the capital planning region. I commend the foresight shown by Reeve, Brad, Erd and the council of the Romanist's Palli of McDonald in exercising their authority openly and with common sense. From the mid-20th century onward, Manitoba has periodically pursued forms of regional centralization intended to improve cooperation and coordination amongst municipalities. In each instance, those structures were later reconsidered or unwound when they pulled decision making away from the communities most directly affected. Today, some residents describe current regional planning models using terms such as 15-minute city as shorthand for that reoccurring pattern. not to oppose collaboration but to reflect a historical grounded public concern which makes clarity about authority, accountability and counsel's role essential to any cooperative framework. At the recent Cook's Creek Town Hall, the message was unmistakable. The public expects elected officials to govern, not to be governed by administrative bottlenecks or regional boards. That expectation is being ignored. The WMR is being marketed as collaboration, but the reality is start when it holds effect of veto power, unelected executive steer, the agenda, and springfield is being ushered into this framework without transparent consultation or democratic mandate. At a recent when it paid metropolitan region meeting, the mayor publicly stated that he supports the regional plan, that is not a want, but an imperative And then raise concerns that allowing municipalities to opt out on the legislative timeline, could interfere with the process because it coincides with an election year. He went further by suggesting that the opt out deadline itself should be delayed or altered to give the regional plan more time to proceed. Those views may be held personally, but they were expressed in the wrong form and in the wrong order. Positions of that consequence should be debated openly with Springfield residents first tested through council and only then advanced externally as council directed positions when a mayor articulates such views as at a regional table before they are discussed publicly. Here it is not leadership. It is the exercise of unfettered discretion without mandate. That approach does not protect Springfield's right to choose. It prejudices it. That same logic now underpins Springfield's water to be. A debate that has become the perfect case study of regional dependency creeping in through the back door. It has come to my attention the administration is contemplating a permanent 5% annual levy increase explicitly intended to pressure developers to repay their levies or to prepay their levies with those prepaid funds then used to offset borrowing costs for the water treatment plan. A perpetual compounding levy increases that is disconnected from inflation or actual operating cost is not cost recovery, it is behavioral taxation.
